item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You explain the hook in so much detail that it loses all strenght before collapsing completely on a feeble punch. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Vote withheld; the concept is actually kind of interesting— especially in light of how it could be misused. It doesn't hinge on a weak reference to another SCP, but the implied crossover potential is intriguing.

The biggest flaw is that it's just a WALL of text, and most of it not needed. You could be a lot more concise, and since the concept is so simple the whole article would really benefit from being easier to read. Maybe cut up the paragraphs and simplify the containment/acquisition sections.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't the 110 montauk note belong in the containment procedures section?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if it's doing the same thing as SCP-1191 , just not in the nearby radius? Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: According to the containment, it's allowable to use violent offenders as test subjects. But, "under no circumstances" is testing allowed on subjects without documented violence. That reads to me that they are okay with effectively re-writing reality relating to specific actions, but think that erasing a total person is an unacceptable risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the thing seems to just cover up events, not erase them, Really only anyone near it thinks that it was not preformed by that person. Them still being alive however, contradicts that. So to PROPERLY cover it up, it kills everyone who knows about the procedure.

Theory, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 100% chance of eliminating the need for 110-Montauk. 50% chance of doing it by ending the world, 50% chance through neutralizing the SCP. Probably something they'd try if all else failed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On occasion, testing on human subjects will result in the disappearance of the test subject itself.

Hmmm, maybe the person they killed retroactively came back to life, and retroactively killed them before they had the chance to become a D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have fun trying to get 682 in a 4 m3 volume.

Even if you could get 682 under the water flow, he has no guilt. Hmmm. I haven't written in what I think happens to subjects who both have no guilt and have a verifiable record of violence… Sounds like test log fodder.

Long and short of it is, it ain't pretty, and even if 682 were wiped out, is it cost effective (in terms of the consequences of cleansing that much whatever it is that 1978 removes in the absence of guilt)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keep in mind that 4m3 doesn't mean 4m in every direction. (That would be 64m3.) While 1m*1m*4m would certainly work, so do 1m*2m*2m, 1.33m*1.5m*2m, and so on.

Yeah… yeah, this is what I meant… *quick edit*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that if there is no guilt to remove, it washes the user out of reality completly. That is why some test subjects vanish. But I can't think of why the Foundation would consider that any worse than the usual effect, it's all re-writing history after all.

This is exactly correct. Although, I'm not sure where the Foundation is being evidenced to consider it worse than the usual effect; the washing away guilt was actually discovered second, and only because the Foundation uses D-class personnel, which are mainly violent offenders (death row inmates in particular).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. I had been under the impression that written out that way meant "A cube four meters on a side", if indeed it is "Four cubic meters" then you are correct and my nitpick invalidated.

WHY DON'T YOU LIKE MY NITPICKS!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. I can see why they don't want to test it on the Montauk participants; on the one hand, it might eliminate an XK-level threat. On the other, if it doesn't work out that way…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep in mind that 4m3 doesn't mean 4m in every direction. (That would be 64m3.) While 1m*1m*4m would certainly work, so do 1m*2m*2m, 1.33m*1.5m*2m, and so on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that if there is no guilt to remove, it washes the user out of reality completly. That is why some test subjects vanish. But I can't think of why the Foundation would consider that any worse than the usual effect, it's all re-writing history after all.

There is also this phrase "…his male sibling and female relation by marriage." That is very awkward wording. It's a hard phrase to get right without confusing the relationships; but how about "…his brother and his brother's wife."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a few little nitpicks that may still be picked or nitted (What?) but overall I like this.

The one thing that gets me is the 4-meter-cubed containment area. It specifically has to be in a room that's just over 13 feet tall?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to see this one up - as I mentioned on the IRC I think it could still do with some revision in the Description section to clarify the effect as it's still not hugely clear in the new bit you've added that its most anomalous effect is to wipe all trace of the act from a given radius. That said, the idea is definitely solid and it's much improved as it stands.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People that he failed to kill suddenly show up on record as dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I doubt he feels guilty about anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It's subtle - potential ramifications could be quite severe. What would happen if a mad dictator who initiated a global war used it? Much disruption, especially with record discrepancy between the inside and outside of the zone. This could easily lead to serious sociological and political tensions.

Also, this just begs for a test record. Especially with statements that age plays a role. And why is the requirement for commiting a violent act before using the object present? I've read the description twice and didn't see what would happen otherwise, maybe I missed it.

Overall, it was interesting, made me think, and the photo fits good too. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, and contained, it is kinda boring. It makes me wonder about who has had it in the past and what they've washed away, whether deliberately or not.

Think of it like this: This COULD have caused chaos on the same level as 668, only with the caveat that the chaos would have essentially been erased from history with nobody the wiser. In fact…. that would make a good addendum, some discovered message sent during some lunatic's murder spree, maybe a text or video message somebody sent during the slaughter, and it landed in some server or some… where, far outside of the range of effect. And the note that no such event has been documented as transpiring, ever (EDIT: Other than the "anomalous" video clip/message/whatever).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: tommyleejoneslookingupfromnewspaper.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who wants to see them stick 682 in this thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just feels kind of… boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'd think the Foundation could afford better cameras.

item.title: Some Revisions
item.content:encoded:
Well, apparently, this wasn't as done as I had hoped. Had not intended to publish an unfinished article, but the comments I'm receiving make it clear I should have hacked on this more.

I've removed the censorship on the effective radius throughout the article (excepting where it would help identify █████ International Airport; the exact distance between the original location and the airport was actually determined to be outside the effective radius).

I've removed the censorship around 110-Montauk; hope that doesn't lose me votes, but I tend to agree with others who say it is silly to do.

I've moved the data for the initial acquisition by the Foundation to its own Addendum.

I will be adding a testing log sometime in the next week or so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All right, upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because its only up to a specific range. Outside of the range, it doesn't change anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do we know what this does if it changes both the memories and records of everyone involved?

Withholding my vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And upvote. Nice job here. And good call on the date thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, first article. Got some help in chat while working it up. By the way, removed the redaction on the range. Did not remove the redaction on the date from the updated procedures, because it's kind of extraneous. Someone reviewing the procedures doesn't need to know when they changed, just what they changed to, at least in my opinion. If this is profoundly off, I will change that as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you first posted it, you had "Scp 1978" instead of "SCP-1978". I fixed it. And if you do that, I'll probably upvote, because the idea is interesting.

By the way, good job. I take this is your first article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can you tell me where I messed up the title?

I will remove the redaction to the range contained in the procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but there is no good reason to redact the range of effect in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is actually well-written and a good idea, yet, somehow, you messed up the title and have redaction in the containment procedures.

I'm not voting on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The issue is the secondary effects. If it were just that the shower washes away guilt/sin, that would be one thing. This is a complete erasure of the guilt, and all evidence of the guilt. The gravity of the implications of this to an organization obsessed with information management like the Foundation are what drives the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a very relaxing shower.

I already have one of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note: Under no circumstances are personnel who have participated in any part of Procedure ███-███████ to be allowed to use SCP-1978. -O5-██

I wonder what that could be, maybe it's 110-Montauk.

As for the SCP itself, I found it rather boring, reading it was more of a chore than anything else. It really dragged on and on with all the little details regarding data archives while the main effect is rather simple ("A shower what washes away X" in this case X is guilt/sin.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally, I don't like inter-SCP references. But it is very clear what this is referring to, and I like it.

"Under no circumstances are personnel who have participated in any part of Procedure ███-███████ to be allowed to use SCP-1978. -O5-██."
